quotes.topic: iii7 vi7 II7 V7 derivation method
quotes.verbatim_quote:
The quickest way for you to come up with some good cxamples of this progression is to go back to your iii? vi7 ii7 V7
progressions and raise the 3rd (one fret higher) in the ii? types, thereby making them I]7’s.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Core technique for deriving II7 voicings from ii7 shapes.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
If there are two 3rds in a
ii7 type, try raising the 3rd that is the lower in pitch of the two, and just leave the other one alone; this will create a

117#9 chord in many cases.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specific fingering rule for ambiguous cases; names II7#9 result.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
If you are dealing with a iim7/11 with its 11th in the soprano (highest pitch) then none
of these ideas will work very well.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Boundary condition where conversion method does not apply.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Actually some of them were (that is, the 3rd was raised in the vi7, thereby creating the V17), but most of them
were derived in other ways, specifically to produce more desirable sounds.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Same mechanism applied to vi7 to produce VI7.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
if you are curious, you may wish
to go back to the iii7 vi7 ii7 V7’s and raise the 3rd on the vi7, as explained above, just to see what you come up with.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Self-directed exercise for deriving VI7 voicings.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Remember to try resolving the V7’s to I, occasionally.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Standing rule for practicing V7 resolutions.

quotes.topic: iii7 vi7 II7 V7 chapter scope
quotes.verbatim_quote: The next harmonic formula to concern us is going to be iii? vi7 17 V7 (sometimes 17 will be used in place of ili7).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines chapter's governing harmonic formula and substitution equivalence.
